%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.2-2 Sinusoids in Terms of Exponentials

> **Objective:** Learn to rewrite cosine and sine using complex exponentials — a pattern-recognition tool that unlocks faster algebra in differential equations, phasor analysis, and signal processing.

Whenever a problem contains \(\cos(\phi)\) or \(\sin(\phi)\), rewriting in terms of exponentials is often the fastest algebraic move available. This section shows exactly how cosine and sine emerge directly from Euler's formula — not as a memorized trick, but as a structural consequence of conjugate symmetry. Mastering this pattern will serve you in phasor work, Fourier analysis, and solving linear ODEs. Strong students protect the constants and signs carefully, because that is precisely where easy marks are lost.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Euler's conjugate pair splits cleanly: adding isolates cosine (divide by 2), subtracting isolates sine (divide by 2j). The two warning callouts mark the most common exam errors.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777217125684-4575.png)

## 1. Deriving Cosine and Sine from Euler's Formula

Euler's formula gives us two companion equations:

$$
e^{j\phi} = \cos\phi + j\sin\phi
$$
$$
e^{-j\phi} = \cos\phi - j\sin\phi
$$

Notice that \(e^{-j\phi}\) is simply the complex conjugate of \(e^{j\phi}\) — this conjugate symmetry is the real reason the identities work so cleanly.

### WHY ADDING GIVES COSINE

When you add the two equations, the \(+j\sin\phi\) and \(-j\sin\phi\) terms cancel exactly, leaving \(2\cos\phi\). Cosine is therefore the **symmetric part** — it survives because it appears identically in both expressions.

### WHY SUBTRACTING GIVES SINE

When you subtract the second from the first, the \(\cos\phi\) terms cancel, leaving \(2j\sin\phi\). Sine comes from the **antisymmetric part** — after dividing by \(j\) (and the factor of 2), the imaginary structure collapses to a real result.

> **Key insight:** The pair \(e^{j\phi}\) and \(e^{-j\phi}\) are conjugates. Their sum is always real (cosine), and their difference is always purely imaginary (\(j\) times sine). That conjugate structure is not a coincidence — it is the foundation of every phasor and Fourier argument you will encounter.

$$\cos \phi = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{j\phi}+e^{-j\phi}\right), \qquad \sin \phi = \frac{1}{2j}\left(e^{j\phi}-e^{-j\phi}\right)$$
*Cosine is the average of the two conjugate exponentials (sum divided by 2), while sine is their scaled difference — the subtraction divided by \(2j\) — which strips away the imaginary unit and the factor of 2 introduced by the antisymmetric cancellation.*

## 2. What to Watch for in Exams

Three traps appear repeatedly on exams involving these identities:

### TRAP 1 — FORGETTING THE FACTOR 1/2 IN COSINE

Writing \(\cos\phi = e^{j\phi} + e^{-j\phi}\) without the \(\frac{1}{2}\) is wrong. The sum gives \(2\cos\phi\), so the factor of \(\frac{1}{2}\) is mandatory.

### TRAP 2 — USING 1/2 INSTEAD OF 1/(2j) FOR SINE

Sine requires division by \(2j\), not \(2\). The subtraction yields \(2j\sin\phi\), so you must divide by \(2j\) to recover \(\sin\phi\). Writing \(\frac{1}{2}\) here gives a result that is off by a factor of \(j\).

### TRAP 3 — REVERSING THE SUBTRACTION ORDER

The sine formula requires \(e^{j\phi} - e^{-j\phi}\). Reversing to \(e^{-j\phi} - e^{j\phi}\) silently introduces a minus sign, giving \(-\sin\phi\) instead.

### QUICK SELF-CHECK

> Replace \(\phi\) with \(-\phi\) in your expression. If it is cosine, the result must be **unchanged** (cosine is even). If it is sine, the result must **flip sign** (sine is odd). Any formula that fails this test contains an error.

Think of \(e^{j\phi}\) and \(e^{-j\phi}\) as two mirror-image rotating signals — one spinning counterclockwise, the other clockwise at the same rate. Each carries a horizontal component (cosine) and a vertical component (sine), but the vertical components point in opposite directions. When you add the two signals, the opposing vertical parts cancel and the shared horizontal part doubles, leaving pure cosine. When you subtract one from the other, the horizontal parts cancel and the vertical parts reinforce — but since those vertical parts carry a factor of \(j\), you divide by \(j\) (and the factor of 2) to recover the real-valued sine. The mirror symmetry is not decorative; it is the exact mechanism that separates the two trigonometric functions.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- \(\cos\phi = \frac{1}{2}(e^{j\phi}+e^{-j\phi})\) — symmetric sum of conjugate exponentials, divided by 2
- \(\sin\phi = \frac{1}{2j}(e^{j\phi}-e^{-j\phi})\) — antisymmetric difference divided by \(2j\), not \(2\)
- Self-check: replace \(\phi\) with \(-\phi\) — cosine must stay unchanged, sine must flip sign

*In the next section we will move from sinusoidal identities to sketching useful signals.*
